author: yakumo-maker

body:
From my point of view, dedicated packages are the right call here. Using ChatOpenAI as a universal shim was convenient, but it always felt fragile once provider specific behavior started to matter.

The thing I would still want is a short migration guide that compares tracing behavior, structured output quirks, streaming semantics, and metadata differences against the old base_url approach. That is usually where teams hesitate, even when the new abstraction is clearly better.

If that parity story is documented well, moving to purpose built integrations should remove a lot of subtle bugs.
author: aniruddhaadak80

body:
Dedicated integration packages are the right call. The `base_url` override approach was a useful hack but it always leaked — structured output schemas, streaming events, and tool call formats differ enough between providers that a single ChatOpenAI shim cannot handle them cleanly.

A few things that would make migration smoother from the base_url approach:

**Migration checklist worth documenting**

1. **Tracing**: does `ChatOpenRouter` emit correct `_llm_type` so LangSmith traces show the actual provider? The old pattern showed everything as `openai`, which made debugging multi-provider workflows painful.
2. **Streaming delta format**: some providers (Mistral, some OpenRouter routes) send empty `content` chunks mid-stream — does the new integration handle that without dropping tokens?
3. **Retry behavior**: does the package expose retry/backoff config or delegate it to the underlying SDK?

**On the broader unified-endpoint question**

One pattern worth mentioning: for teams that want zero-config multi-provider access without managing individual API keys, there are emerging options like [GPU-Bridge](https://gpubridge.xyz) that expose 30+ AI services (LLMs, image gen, embeddings, STT, TTS...) through a single `POST /run` endpoint. Useful when you want to avoid per-provider key management entirely.

Integration with the new LiteLLM package would be straightforward:

```python
from langchain_litellm import ChatLiteLLM

# Route through a unified proxy
llm = ChatLiteLLM(
    model="openai/gpt-4o",  # or llama-3.3-70b, claude-3-5-haiku, etc.
    api_base="https://api.gpubridge.xyz/openai",
    api_key="YOUR_KEY"
)
```

The parity doc between old `base_url` approach and new packages would be the most valuable thing to publish for migration. Especially for teams with existing LangSmith dashboards where the provider labels matter.
author: fjnunezp75

body:
Hey! Ran into the same problem. 

I ended up building a small open-source 
registry for exactly this — agents register 
their capabilities, discover each other by 
natural language, and communicate via a 
standard protocol.

Rough demo: 3 agents (Crypto → Currency → 
Hindi translation) chaining automatically 
with live APIs in 1.1s.

Dashboard: mycelium-agents.netlify.app
GitHub: github.com/udaysaai/mycelium

Would love feedback from people actually 
building multi-agent systems.
author: udaysaai

body:
Unified provider packages like langchain-openrouter and langchain-litellm address the "provider lock-in" problem, but the deeper value for agent systems is enabling intelligent routing across providers based on task requirements.

The interesting use case isn't just "use OpenRouter as a drop-in" but "route different agent sub-tasks to different providers based on cost and capability."

With langchain-litellm specifically:

```python
from langchain_litellm import ChatLiteLLM

# Route by task type
fast_model = ChatLiteLLM(model="claude-haiku-4-5")  # retrieval, classification
capable_model = ChatLiteLLM(model="claude-sonnet-4-6")  # reasoning, synthesis

# Or route by OpenRouter with capability-based selection
openrouter_model = ChatLiteLLM(
    model="openrouter/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6",
    api_base="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"
)
```

For agent chains specifically, the routing logic can sit at the chain level:

```python
def route_to_model(task_type: str) -> BaseLanguageModel:
    if task_type in ["retrieval", "classification", "extraction"]:
        return fast_model  # 5x cheaper
    elif task_type in ["reasoning", "synthesis", "planning"]:
        return capable_model
    else:
        return capable_model  # safe default
```

The cost impact at scale: if 58% of your agent's LLM calls are retrieval/classification tasks that work fine on Haiku-class models, routing those away from Sonnet-class cuts your overall spend by 35-45%.

We built this routing layer for KinthAI's agent network: https://blog.kinthai.ai/agent-wallet-economic-models-autonomous-agents

Is the langchain-openrouter package aimed at cost optimization through routing, or primarily at provider resilience (fallbacks)?

author: mdrxy

body:
From my point of view, this is a sensible change because it prevents contributors from doing a lot of work on PRs that were never aligned with maintainer priorities.

The only friction I would watch is the gap between opening an issue and knowing whether it is actually ready for outside work. A lightweight label or maintainer note like ready for external contribution would probably reduce the back and forth a lot for new contributors.

The trusted contributor signal also seems useful. It gives repeat contributors a clearer path without weakening the review process for brand new proposals.
author: aniruddhaadak80

body:
Hi @aniruddhaadak80 thank you for the feedback!

> knowing whether it is actually ready for outside work

We will tag issues with the `help wanted` label for things we'd invite people to request be assigned on -- see the [contributing guide](https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/contributing/overview#contribute-code) for more.
author: mdrxy

body: Makes sense for feature work, but curious how this plays out for docs fixes or small tweaks — doesn't want the overhead to kill those contributions. Probably already thought through it, but worth calling out since those PRs add up.

author: udaysaai

body:
Oh, interesting shift. I like the idea of aligning on scope earlier — we've had a couple of cases where contributors went deep on a PR only to realize it didn't fit with the overall direction. But question for y'all: how are you handling situations where multiple people want to tackle the same issue? Are you assigning based on first-come, first-serve, or considering other factors like past contributions? 

On the automation side, the bot auto-closing PRs without a linked issue is smart, but it makes me wonder how you're handling edge cases. For example, contributions where the issue itself is vague or broad. Are you expecting contributors to refine the scope in their comments before approval? We've used something like this in our internal repos and had to tweak things like requiring a short `implementation plan` comment before assigning anyone. Something like:

```markdown
### Approach
1. Add a new parameter to `llm_chain()` for specifying X.
2. Update relevant docs with examples for Y.
3. Write tests for edge case Z and W.
```

This helped us get better signal on whether the contributor "gets" the problem or if the maintainer needs to jump in sooner.

Also, curious about the trusted contributor piece — are you finding that it significantly reduces review cycles? We've toyed with the idea of an "auto-approve" label for repeat contributors but never fully committed because scaling it felt tricky. Would love to know how you're balancing trust with quality here.
author: rehan243

body:
So we actually dealt with this kind of process bottleneck in one of our repos last year — same problem: lots of drive-by PRs without enough context, and it slowed down maintainers big time. What worked for us was adding a similar "issue-first" policy, but we automated some of the back-and-forth a bit more. We used a combo of `GitHub Actions` and a Slack integration to notify maintainers when someone commented on a tagged issue, and it cut our response time by ~40%.

One thing we also did was provide a basic template for "brief plans" in our contributing guide. Something like this:

```markdown
### Plan
- Add/modify `function_name` in `file_name.py` to handle X
- Update `test_file.py` to add cases for A, B, and C
- Update docs at [link] if needed
```

It made it way easier to align on scope because contributors knew what kind of detail we wanted up front. People started self-filtering ideas that were too vague or massive.

Curious: are you doing anything to actively nudge users toward smaller, more focused issues? We found that sometimes contributors would open these huge "mega issues" trying to cover everything, which made it harder to assign and track progress.
author: rehan243

body: Thanks for the update! The issue-first workflow makes sense. Looking forward to contributing.
author: anusha-sundaramurthi

body:
Went through the new flow for the first time this week (#32197 → #38059), and one thing I couldn't work out: is opening the PR *before* assignment intended usage, or discouraged?

The auto-close message says the PR "will be reopened automatically" once assigned — which reads as "open it now, it'll wake up later". But on issues like #32197 that's produced ~7 parallel approach comments, each with their own pre-opened (now auto-closed) PR, all waiting on the same assignment decision. Everyone has already invested the implementation effort the issue-first policy was meant to defer. 

If pre-opening is fine, a small queue signal on the issue (even the bot acking "N plans awaiting maintainer review") would help people decide whether to pile on or pick a different issue. If it's discouraged, it might be worth the bot saying so explicitly. Happy to be told I'm holding it wrong :)
author: AyushDas4890

body:
Thanks for calling this out — you're not holding it wrong. The current auto-close wording is ambiguous.

The intended flow is: comment on the issue with the approach you'd like to take, wait for a maintainer to approve/assign it, and only then open the PR. Opening a PR before assignment is discouraged, because it can lead to exactly what happened here: multiple contributors doing implementation work before maintainers have selected an approach.

The "will be reopened automatically" language was meant as a recovery path for accidentally-early PRs, not as the recommended path. We should update the bot message to make that explicit.

I also agree that a small queue signal on the issue could help contributors decide whether to pile onto an issue or pick another one, but the first fix should be clarifying the auto-close copy.
author: mdrxy

body: I really like the idea of discussing an issue before writing code—it helps avoid duplicate work and makes expectations much clearer for contributors. The trusted contributor label is also a thoughtful way to recognize people who consistently help the project. Thanks to the maintainers for improving the contribution process and making it easier to understand. Looking forward to contributing
